title: Shoe cleaning paste
subtitle: A bicarbonate of soda and castile soap paste for cleaning canvas, rubber, and fabric shoes
excerpt: A thick paste of bicarbonate of soda, castile soap, and a little water cleans canvas trainers, rubber soles, and fabric shoes effectively without the bleaching action of hydrogen peroxide. Applied with an old toothbrush, left briefly, then wiped and rinsed. Suitable for canvas and fabric uppers, rubber soles, and mesh. Not for leather or suede.
sourceNotes: Bicarbonate of soda as a mild abrasive and deodoriser: sodium bicarbonate particle size and abrasive cleaning action, standard household cleaning reference. Castile soap as a surfactant base for pastes: standard formulation reference. This paste is safe for canvas, rubber, and synthetic mesh; it is not suitable for leather (the bicarbonate is too alkaline for finished leather) or suede (liquid damages the nap).

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 small batch (cleans 1 to 2 pairs of shoes)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix fresh each time; the paste dries out on standing.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Mix fresh and use immediately. Do not store; the paste dries and loses its texture within an hour.

glossaryTerms.definition: A surface-active agent that reduces surface tension and lifts grease and grime. Castile soap is the surfactant in this paste.

content.text: Canvas trainers and fabric shoes pick up scuffs, mud, and grime that resists wiping alone. Bicarbonate of soda and castile soap make a mild abrasive and
paste: the bicarbonate scrubs and deodorises the fabric; the castile soap lifts grease. Use on canvas, synthetic uppers, and rubber soles only. Do not use on leather or suede.

items.prepNote: 2 teaspoons; adjust to get a thick paste consistency

content.text: Combine the bicarbonate of soda, castile soap, and water in a small bowl and stir to a thick paste. The consistency should be similar to toothpaste: thick enough to stay on a vertical surface, but spreadable. Add a few more drops of water if too stiff.

Remove laces before cleaning. Apply the paste to the fabric upper and rubber sole using a soft toothbrush. Work in circular motions, paying extra attention to the welt (the join between sole and upper) where dirt accumulates. Leave the paste in place for 5 minutes to let the bicarbonate work. Wipe off with a damp cloth, then rinse the shoe surface with a cloth wrung out in clean water. Stuff loosely with newspaper and leave to dry at room temperature. Do not dry near a direct heat source.

White canvas uppers may benefit from a light rinse under running water after wiping. Coloured canvas should not be over-wetted; test on a hidden area if the colour fastness is uncertain. For rubber soles and toe caps, the paste can be left slightly longer (10 minutes) and scrubbed with more pressure.
